message:
fix(mcp_tools): wa.workflow_run dry-run routes through authorize_preview + skips audit write

Three coordinated adjustments inside `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs`.

## 1. authorize_mcp_policy_call helper

New private helper:

    fn authorize_mcp_policy_call(
        engine: &mut PolicyEngine,
        input:  &PolicyInput,
        dry_run: bool,
    ) -> PolicyDecision {
        if dry_run {
            engine.authorize_preview(input)
        } else {
            engine.authorize(input)
        }
    }

picks the preview vs live policy path based on the `dry_run` bit. The
preview variant (landed in the prior policy.rs commit) evaluates the
decision without spending a slot from the shared rate-limit window —
so a Codex-driven dry-run probe of `wa.workflow_run` no longer
consumes WorkflowRun budget, and a caller who runs three previews
followed by one real workflow still sees the real workflow get an
Allowed decision that actually charges the rate limiter.

## 2. WaWorkflowRunTool::call honors dry_run on the policy path

The tool's live-path block was doing

    let decision = policy_engine.authorize(&input);
    if decision.is_denied() { … persist_mcp_policy_denial_async(…).await; … }
    if decision.requires_approval() { … mint approval token … }

which meant:

- dry_run probes went through the non-preview `authorize` (budget
  bleed);
- dry_run denials were persisted to the policy_denied_audit table
  as if the user had actually tried to run the workflow;
- dry_run "requires_approval" outcomes silently fell through to
  approval-token minting, which side-effects an on-disk token.

Fix:

- `authorize` replaced with `authorize_mcp_policy_call(…, params.dry_run)`,
  so `policy_engine.authorize_preview(&input)` runs when dry_run is
  set.
- The `persist_mcp_policy_denial_async` call is now gated by
  `if !params.dry_run { … }` — previews do not dirty the
  policy_denied_audit table.
- The `decision.requires_approval()` branch short-circuits for
  dry_run and returns

        Err(McpToolError::new(
            MCP_ERR_POLICY,
            reason,
            Some("Dry-run preview only: rerun without dry_run to
                  request an allow-once approval token.".to_string()),
        ))

  so a preview surfaces "this *would* require approval; here is how
  you get one" without ever creating the approval artifact.

## 3. Regression test

New `dry_run_policy_authorization_does_not_consume_workflow_rate_limit_budget`:

- Builds a `Config` with `rate_limit_per_pane = 1`, mints a shared
  `Arc<Mutex<RateLimiter>>` via `build_mcp_shared_rate_limiter(&cfg)`,
  and constructs a `PolicyEngine` through
  `build_policy_engine_with_shared_rate_limiter(&cfg,
  require_prompt_active, Arc::clone(&shared_rate_limiter))` so the
  test sees exactly the production limiter wiring.
- Fires three dry_run workflow authorizations (all must be Allowed).
- Previews the rate-limiter directly against the shared instance —
  must still be `is_allowed()` (no budget consumed).
- Fires one live workflow authorization (must be Allowed — this is
  the first real call).
- Previews again — must be `!is_allowed()` (the live call did spend
  the single slot in the window).

This locks in the contract both at the policy-authorize layer
(`authorize_preview` is non-mutating) and at the tool-handler layer
(`authorize_mcp_policy_call` actually uses it for dry_run).

## 4. Clippy collateral

- The `Content` content-body extraction in `parse_json_content`
  switches from `match &contents[0] { Content::Text { text } => … , _
  => panic!("expected text content") // ubs:ignore }` to a
  `matches!` + `let Some(Content::Text { text }) = contents.first()
  else { return serde_json::Value::Null; };` pair. Same failure
  semantic (assertion first, then the else-path ensures an
  infallible `text` binding for the `serde_json::from_str` call),
  but clippy no longer flags the bare-panic match arm. Test-only.

- `resolve_row.unwrap_or_else(|| panic!("missing audit row for
  {action_kind}"))` → `.expect("missing audit row for requested
  action kind")`. Test helper; clippy `expect_fun_call`.

- `acquire_mcp_tx_contract_lock` second-acquisition assertion
  switches from `match super::…(…) { Ok(_) => panic!("…"), Err(err)
  => err }` to `assert!(second.is_err(), "…")` + `let Err(err) =
  second else { return; };`. Same "fail if Ok" semantic in test
  context; clippy preferred shape.

- Test import list picks up `authorize_mcp_policy_call` and
  `build_policy_engine_with_shared_rate_limiter` so the new
  regression above compiles.

Closes the dry_run-bleeds-the-limiter vector for the workflow
surface; wa.send already landed the same treatment in an earlier
commit in this series.
